quiz.title: Unit 5 Quiz 8, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Nomenclature and Formula Writing Practice
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Naming Ionic Compounds, Transition Metals, Polyatomic Ions, Formula Writing, Stock System

questions.question: Name the compound $\text{MgC}_2\text{O}_4$:
questions.explanation: $\text{MgC}_2\text{O}_4$ is magnesium oxalate. It contains the oxalate ion ($\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}$). Magnesium (Group 2) forms only one type of ion (+2), so no Roman numeral is needed. Magnesium carbonate (A) would be $\text{MgCO}_3$.

questions.question: Name the compound $\text{SnCl}_2$:
questions.explanation: $\text{SnCl}_2$ is named tin(II) chloride. Chlorine has a -1 charge, so two chlorines give -2, meaning tin has a +2 charge, indicated by (II). Tin(IV) chloride would be $\text{SnCl}_4$.

questions.question: What is the formula for Barium Sulfide?
questions.explanation: Barium sulfide is $\text{BaS}$. Barium (Group 2) forms $\text{Ba}^{2+}$, and sulfide is $\text{S}^{2-}$, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio. $\text{BaSO}_4$ (C) is barium sulfate.

questions.question: Name the compound: $\text{Fe}_3\text{P}_2$
questions.explanation: $\text{Fe}_3\text{P}_2$ is named iron(II) phosphide. Phosphorus ($\text{P}^{3-}$) has a -3 charge, so two phosphorus give -6. Therefore, three irons total +6, meaning each iron has a +2 charge, indicated by (II).

questions.question: What is the formula for Zinc Hydroxide?
questions.explanation: Zinc hydroxide is $\text{Zn(OH)}_2$. Zinc typically forms $\text{Zn}^{2+}$, and hydroxide is $\text{OH}^-$ with a -1 charge, so two hydroxides are needed.

questions.question: What is the formula for FrBr?
questions.explanation: $\text{FrBr}$ is francium bromide. Francium (Group 1) forms $\text{Fr}^+$, and bromide is $\text{Br}^-$, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio. The question asks for the formula, but the options are names. $\text{FrBr}$ is named francium bromide.

questions.question: What is the name for $\text{CuCl}$?
questions.explanation: $\text{CuCl}$ is named copper(I) chloride. Chlorine has a -1 charge, so copper must have a +1 charge, indicated by (I). Copper(II) chloride would be $\text{CuCl}_2$, and cupric chloride is an older name for $\text{CuCl}_2$.

questions.question: What is the formula for Copper(II) sulfide?
questions.explanation: Copper(II) sulfide is $\text{CuS}$. Copper(II) has a +2 charge ($\text{Cu}^{2+}$), and sulfide is $\text{S}^{2-}$ with a -2 charge, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio. $\text{Cu}_2\text{S}$ (B) would be copper(I) sulfide.

questions.question: What is the formula for Silver(I) chloride?
questions.explanation: Silver(I) chloride is $\text{AgCl}$. Silver(I) has a +1 charge ($\text{Ag}^+$), and chloride is $\text{Cl}^-$ with a -1 charge, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio. Silver does not commonly form other charges, so the (I) is often omitted, but the formula remains $\text{AgCl}$.

questions.question: What is the formula for Copper(I) Oxide?
questions.explanation: Copper(I) oxide is $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$. Copper(I) has a +1 charge ($\text{Cu}^+$), and oxide is $\text{O}^{2-}$, so two copper ions are needed to balance the charge.

questions.question: What is the chemical name for $\text{MgSO}_3$?
questions.explanation: $\text{MgSO}_3$ is magnesium sulfite. It contains the sulfite ion ($\text{SO}_3^{2-}$). Magnesium sulfate (A) would be $\text{MgSO}_4$, and magnesium sulfide (C) would be $\text{MgS}$.

questions.question: What is the formula for Aluminum Chlorate?
questions.explanation: Aluminum chlorate is $\text{Al(ClO}_3)_3$. Aluminum forms $\text{Al}^{3+}$, and chlorate is $\text{ClO}_3^-$ with a -1 charge, so three chlorates are needed. $\text{AlCl}_3$ (D) would be aluminum chloride.

questions.question: What is the formula for magnesium sulfate?
questions.explanation: Magnesium sulfate is $\text{MgSO}_4$. Magnesium ($\text{Mg}^{2+}$) combines with sulfate ($\text{SO}_4^{2-}$) in a 1:1 ratio. $\text{MgS}$ (A) is magnesium sulfide, and $\text{MgSO}_3$ (B) is magnesium sulfite.

questions.question: What is the formula for iron(III) phosphate?
questions.explanation: Iron(III) phosphate is $\text{FePO}_4$. Iron(III) has a +3 charge ($\text{Fe}^{3+}$), and phosphate is $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$ with a -3 charge, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio. $\text{Fe}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2$ (B) would be iron(II) phosphate.

questions.question: What is the formula for potassium carbonate?
questions.explanation: Potassium carbonate is $\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3$. Potassium ($\text{K}^+$) has a +1 charge, and carbonate is $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ with a -2 charge, so two potassium ions are needed.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered nomenclature and formula writing! 🧪📝
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review polyatomic ion charges and transition metal oxidation states.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on distinguishing between similar ions like -ate and -ite.
scoreMessages.0: Please review naming rules for ionic compounds, including those with polyatomic ions and transition metals.
